1:
On Jude 1 — “Jude, the servant of Jesus Christ, and brother of James, to them that are sanctified by God the Father, and preserved in Jesus Christ, and called” — the witnesses mark the writer and the threefold blessing of his readers.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the author. Robertson: “servant” (doulos) as James (James 1:1); “brother of James” — thus Jude identifies himself, not as “Judas of James” (Luke 6:16). Barnes notes he “does not call himself an apostle.” The Puritan Henry: he was “name-sake… out of whose loins… the Messiah came.” The Reformed Calvin: “servant of Christ” here “with respect to his apostleship.”

The verse opens the letter with the author’s humble title and a rich description of his readers. Jude calls himself simply Christ’s servant and James’s brother; his readers he marks as those sanctified by the Father, preserved in Christ, and called.

So the witnesses see Jude open his brief but weighty letter. The witnesses note the modesty of his self-designation: not “apostle” nor “brother of the Lord,” but “servant of Jesus Christ” — the highest glory, Wesley says, that any can aspire to — and “brother of James,” the well-known leader of the Jerusalem church. They mark the threefold blessing on the readers, which will undergird the whole letter’s call to perseverance: they are sanctified (set apart and made holy) by the Father’s love, preserved (kept safe) in Christ, and effectually called by grace. Against the apostasy the letter will expose, this opening grounds the faithful in God’s keeping. The verse thus frames Jude’s purpose: writing to a beloved people whom God has set apart, guarded, and summoned, he will rouse them to contend for the faith and to keep themselves — even as God keeps them — against the ungodly intruders he is about to unmask.

So the witnesses see the letter open under the sign of God’s keeping. The modesty of “servant of Jesus Christ” — the highest glory, Wesley says — and “brother of James” set the tone of humility; and the threefold blessing grounds the faithful before the storm: sanctified by the Father’s love, preserved in Christ, effectually called by grace. Against the apostasy the letter will expose, the saints are framed at the outset as a people whom God has set apart, guarded, and summoned — secure in his hold even as Jude rouses them to contend.
2:
On Jude 2 — “Mercy unto you, and peace, and love, be multiplied” — the witnesses mark the salutation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the form. Robertson: “be multiplied” (plethyntheie, optative) as in 1 Peter 1:2; 2 Peter 1:2. Clarke: “mercy — for even the best have no merit… peace — with God and your consciences… love… be unboundedly increased.” The Reformed Calvin: “mercy means nearly the same as grace… grace is properly the effect of mercy.”

The verse pronounces a threefold blessing. Jude prays that mercy, peace, and love may be multiplied to his readers — not merely given, but increased without measure.

So the witnesses see in Jude’s greeting a prayer for the choicest blessings, abundantly enlarged. The witnesses note the three gifts: mercy, the pardon and pity of God toward needy sinners who have no merit of their own; peace, with God and in the conscience, flowing from that mercy; and love, both God’s love to them and theirs to God and one another. They mark Calvin’s observation that mercy is the fountain and grace its effect — God embraces us because he pities our misery. The prayer that these be “multiplied” asks not a bare measure but an overflowing increase. The verse thus sets a tone of grace at the letter’s outset, even before its stern warnings: Jude longs for his readers’ abundant good. Against the loveless, graceless intruders he will describe, he opens by invoking mercy, peace, and love — the very atmosphere of the gospel — to be enlarged in the hearts of the faithful, that they may stand secure amid the dangers to come.

So the witnesses see in Jude’s greeting a prayer for the choicest blessings, abundantly enlarged. Mercy is the fountain — God’s pity toward sinners who have no merit; peace flows from it, with God and in the conscience; and love crowns it, God’s to us and ours to him and to one another. They mark Calvin’s note that grace is the effect of mercy. The prayer that these be “multiplied” asks not a bare measure but an overflowing increase — the very atmosphere of the gospel breathed over the faithful before the stern warnings begin.
3:
On Jude 3 — “Beloved… it was needful for me to write unto you, and exhort you that ye should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints” — the witnesses mark the great purpose of the letter.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the occasion. Robertson: “all diligence” (pasan spouden) as in 2 Peter 1:5; “common salvation” (Titus 1:4); “I was constrained” — “I had necessity.” The Puritan Henry: the design was to “establish them in the Christian faith… especially in times of notorious opposition.” Wesley: contend “earnestly — yet humbly, meekly, and lovingly.”

The verse states Jude’s aim. Intending to write of their common salvation, he found it necessary instead to urge them to contend earnestly for the faith once for all delivered to the saints. A present danger redirected his pen.

So the witnesses see Jude announce the famous charge that defines his letter. The witnesses note the change of plan: he had meant to write of the shared joys of salvation, but the peril of false teachers compelled a more urgent word — to “contend earnestly” for the faith. They mark the key phrases: the faith “once delivered” — given once for all, a fixed deposit not to be altered or added to; and “to the saints” — entrusted to the whole church to guard. The contending is to be vigorous, a strenuous striving as for a contest; yet, Wesley urges, humble, meek, and loving, lest the defence of truth breed an unchristian temper. The verse thus issues the church’s perennial summons: to hold fast and defend the apostolic gospel against corruption, with earnestness and grace together. It prepares the reason Jude will now give — the intrusion of ungodly men who threaten that very faith.

So the witnesses see Jude announce the charge that defines his letter. He had meant to write of the shared joys of salvation, but a present peril compelled a more urgent word — to contend earnestly for the faith. They mark the key phrases: the faith “once delivered,” a fixed deposit given once for all; and “to the saints,” entrusted to the whole church to guard. The striving is to be vigorous, yet (Wesley) humble, meek, and loving, lest the defence of truth breed an unchristian temper — the church’s perennial summons to hold fast and defend the apostolic gospel.
4:
On Jude 4 — “For there are certain men crept in unawares… ungodly men, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness, and denying the only Lord God, and our Lord Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the intruders and their character.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the men. Robertson: “crept in” (pareisedysan), “to slip in secretly as if by a side door”; “set forth” (progegrammenoi), written of beforehand. Clarke: “long ago proscribed, and condemned.” Barnes parallels 2 Peter 2:1. The Reformed Calvin: Satan “now… attacks and harasses you in a peculiar manner; it is therefore necessary to take up arms.”

The verse gives the reason for contending: false teachers have secretly entered the church. Ungodly men, long ago marked for condemnation, have crept in — men who pervert God’s grace into licence and deny the Lord himself.

So the witnesses see Jude unmask the peril that prompted his letter. The witnesses note the stealth of the intruders — they “crept in unawares,” slipping by a side door under fair pretences, not openly avowed enemies but covert corrupters within. Their character is twofold and damning: they turn the grace of God into “lasciviousness,” making free pardon a pretext for unbridled sin; and they “deny” the Lord, by doctrine or by a life that disowns him. They mark that such men were “before of old ordained” to condemnation — their judgment long since written in God’s word and foretold. Calvin sees in this a special assault of Satan demanding the church take up arms. The verse thus identifies the enemy the whole letter will denounce: antinomian deceivers who abuse grace and deny Christ, against whom the saints must contend. It prepares the solemn examples of judgment Jude will marshal to show that such ungodliness never escapes God’s vengeance.

So the witnesses see Jude unmask the peril that prompted his letter. The intruders “crept in unawares,” slipping by a side door under fair pretences — covert corrupters within, not avowed enemies. Their character is twofold and damning: they turn grace into licence, and deny the Lord by doctrine or by life. They were “before of old ordained” to condemnation, their judgment long since written and foretold. Calvin sees a special assault of Satan demanding the church take up arms. The verse identifies the enemy the whole letter denounces — antinomian deceivers against whom the saints must contend.
5:
On Jude 5 — “I will therefore put you in remembrance, though ye once knew this, how that the Lord, having saved the people out of the land of Egypt, afterward destroyed them that believed not” — the witnesses mark the first example of judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the recall. Robertson: “though ye know all things once for all” (eidotas hapax panta); some manuscripts read “the Lord”, some “Jesus.” Clarke discusses “once” (hapax). The Reformed Calvin: Jude “adduced nothing new… that what he was going to say might gain more credit.” The Puritan Henry: “none ought to presume on their privileges… even they perished in unbelief.”

The verse gives the first of three solemn warnings from history. The Lord saved Israel out of Egypt, yet afterward destroyed those who did not believe. Deliverance did not secure the unbelieving from judgment.

So the witnesses see Jude begin his catalogue of God’s judgments with Israel in the wilderness. The witnesses note that he appeals to what the readers already knew, reminding rather than informing, that the weight of familiar Scripture might press his warning home. The lesson is grave: the very people whom God redeemed from Egypt by mighty wonders, who ate the manna and saw the sea divided, were nonetheless destroyed in the desert for their unbelief. They mark Henry’s point that outward privilege and apparent conversion cannot secure those who fall into unbelief and disobedience; none may presume on past mercies. Wesley adds the warning — let none rest on former grace as if now out of danger. The verse thus sets the pattern Jude will press: that God spares not the ungodly, however favoured. The first example — redeemed Israel destroyed for unbelief — warns the intruders and steadies the faithful, showing that profession without persevering faith ends in ruin.

So the witnesses see Jude begin his catalogue of judgments with Israel in the wilderness. He appeals to what the readers already knew, that familiar Scripture might press the warning home. The lesson is grave: the very people God redeemed from Egypt were destroyed in the desert for unbelief. They mark Henry’s point that outward privilege cannot secure those who fall into unbelief; none may presume on past mercies. Wesley adds — let none rest on former grace as if now out of danger. The first example warns the intruders and steadies the faithful: profession without persevering faith ends in ruin.
6:
On Jude 6 — “And the angels which kept not their first estate, but left their own habitation, he hath reserved in everlasting chains under darkness unto the judgment of the great day” — the witnesses mark the fall of the angels.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson and Barnes: “first estate” (arche, principality, pre-eminence); “kept not” their dominion (cf. 2 Peter 2:4). Wesley: they “voluntarily left their own habitation… reserved… in everlasting chains under darkness.” The Reformed Calvin: “an argument from the greater to the less… God punished their defection… he will not then forgive our perfidy.”

The verse gives the second example. The angels who did not keep their exalted place, but forsook their proper dwelling, God has bound in everlasting chains under darkness, awaiting the judgment of the great day. The highest creatures did not escape.

So the witnesses see Jude press his warning higher, to the fallen angels. The witnesses note the dignity these creatures forfeited: they “kept not their first estate” — their principality, the lofty rank and habitation God gave them — but abandoned it through pride and rebellion. For this God has consigned them to “everlasting chains under darkness,” held fast for the judgment of the last day. They mark Calvin’s argument from the greater to the less: if God spared not angels, beings far above us, when they sinned, how shall he spare men who depart from his grace? The fall and reservation of these heavenly powers prove that no exaltation of nature exempts from judgment. The verse thus deepens the lesson of Israel’s destruction: rebellion against God brings inescapable doom, even on angels. It warns the intruders, who themselves had forsaken their place, that the same chains and darkness await all who keep not their allegiance to God.

So the witnesses see Jude press the warning higher, to the fallen angels. They forfeited their “first estate” — their principality and habitation — through pride, and God has consigned them to everlasting chains under darkness, held for the judgment of the last day. They mark Calvin’s argument from the greater to the less: if God spared not angels, beings far above us, how shall he spare men who depart from his grace? The fall and reservation of these heavenly powers prove that no exaltation of nature exempts from judgment — a warning to the intruders, who had themselves forsaken their place.
7:
On Jude 7 — “Even as Sodom and Gomorrha, and the cities about them… are set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal fire” — the witnesses mark the third example.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the cities and the type. Robertson: the cities of the plain, “Admah and Zeboiim” (Deuteronomy 29:23; Hosea 11:8), Zoar spared (cf. 2 Peter 2:6). Wesley: their vengeance “is an example or a type of eternal fire.” The Reformed Calvin: God “punishes… all the ungodly… the fire… was a type of the eternal fire.”

The verse gives the third warning. Sodom, Gomorrah, and the neighbouring cities, given over to gross immorality, are set forth as an example, suffering the vengeance of eternal fire. Their fate stands as a type of the judgment to come.

So the witnesses see Jude complete his triad of judgments with the cities of the plain. The witnesses note the third historic instance — after redeemed Israel and the exalted angels, the prosperous and fruitful cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, destroyed for their unnatural lusts. They mark that these are “set forth for an example” — a public, lasting warning — and that their fiery overthrow was a “type of eternal fire,” a temporal foreshadowing of the everlasting judgment awaiting the ungodly. Calvin observes that God here punishes all the ungodly without distinction, sparing none. The verse thus rounds out Jude’s argument from history: three times God has visited terrible judgment on rebellion — in the wilderness, among the angels, and on the cities of the plain — each a witness that ungodliness is not overlooked. The intruders who turn grace into licence stand under the same sentence; the smoking ruin of Sodom is the standing emblem of the fire that awaits all who give themselves over to sin and deny the Lord.

So the witnesses see Jude complete his triad of judgments with the cities of the plain. After redeemed Israel and the exalted angels come the prosperous cities of Sodom and Gomorrah, destroyed for their unnatural lusts. They mark that these are “set forth for an example” — a public, lasting warning — their fiery overthrow a “type of eternal fire.” Three times God has visited terrible judgment on rebellion — in the wilderness, among the angels, on the cities of the plain — each a witness that ungodliness is not overlooked. The smoking ruin of Sodom is the standing emblem of the fire awaiting all who deny the Lord.
8:
On Jude 8 — “Likewise also these filthy dreamers defile the flesh, despise dominion, and speak evil of dignities” — the witnesses mark the threefold sin of the intruders.

The grammatical-historical commentators apply the examples. Robertson: “in their dreamings” (enypniazomenoi, to dream), the false teachers of verse 4 — “in spite of these warnings.” The Puritan Henry: “sin is filthiness… delusion is a dream.” Wesley: they “despise authority… rail at dignities,” the holy overseers of the flock. Clarke: as unholy as Sodom, as rebellious as the angels.

The verse turns from the examples to the men they condemn. Like those ancient sinners, these “dreamers” defile their flesh with impurity, despise authority, and revile the honourable. Their sin is threefold — in body, in submission, and in speech.

So the witnesses see Jude apply the judgments of history directly to the intruders. The witnesses note the contemptuous name — “filthy dreamers” — marking both their impurity and their self-deluded fancies, as if they walked through life asleep in their lusts. Their threefold offence answers the three examples: like Sodom they “defile the flesh” with gross immorality; like the rebel angels they “despise dominion,” spurning rightful authority; and they “speak evil of dignities,” reviling those whom God has set in honour. They mark Henry’s diagnosis that sin is filthiness, defiling the man, and delusion a dream that opens the way to all uncleanness. The verse thus paints the character Jude is exposing: men who, despite the plain warnings of God’s past judgments, persist in defiling themselves, scorning order, and railing at the honourable. Their conduct marks them as the very kind whom God has always condemned — and prepares the contrast with Michael’s reverence in the next verse.

So the witnesses see Jude apply the judgments of history directly to the intruders. The contemptuous name — “filthy dreamers” — marks both their impurity and their self-deluded fancies, as if they walked through life asleep in their lusts. Their threefold offence answers the three examples: like Sodom they defile the flesh, like the rebel angels they despise dominion, and they speak evil of dignities. They mark Henry’s diagnosis that sin is filthiness and delusion a dream. Despite the plain warnings of God’s past judgments, these men persist in defiling themselves, scorning order, and railing — marking them as the very kind God has always condemned.
9:
On Jude 9 — “Yet Michael the archangel, when contending with the devil he disputed about the body of Moses, durst not bring against him a railing accusation, but said, The Lord rebuke thee” — the witnesses mark the contrast of angelic reverence.

The grammatical-historical commentators note Michael and the source. Robertson: Michael (Daniel 10:13; Revelation 12:7), “archangel” (also 1 Thessalonians 4:16); “The Lord rebuke thee” recalls Zechariah 3:2. Calvin and Wesley note the story likely from tradition or an apocryphal source, yet true and acknowledged. Gill, by contrast, identifies Michael as Christ himself.

The verse sets a startling contrast. Even Michael the archangel, disputing with the devil over Moses’ body, dared not pronounce a reviling judgment, but referred it to God: “The Lord rebuke thee.” The mightiest angel showed reverence where the intruders show insolence.

So the witnesses see Jude shame the railers by the example of Michael. The witnesses note the force of the contrast: the chief of angels, contending even with Satan himself, would not presume to bring a “railing accusation” but committed the rebuke to God — while these earthly “dreamers” freely revile dignities far above their understanding. They mark the debate over the source — the dispute about Moses’ body is not in the Old Testament, and many take it from tradition or the apocryphal Assumption of Moses, which Jude (or the Spirit through him) cites as a true fact, whether learned by revelation or known to his readers. They note the difference over Michael’s identity: Gill reads him as Christ, “who is as God”; most take him as the created archangel of Daniel and Revelation. Either way, the lesson stands: even the highest holy being uses no insolent speech against the powers of evil, but defers to God’s judgment. The verse thus rebukes the intruders’ arrogant railing by the model of angelic restraint — and exposes, by contrast, how brutish their reviling is.

So the witnesses see Jude shame the railers by the example of Michael. The chief of angels, contending even with Satan himself, would not presume to bring a “railing accusation” but committed the rebuke to God — while these earthly “dreamers” freely revile dignities far above their understanding. They mark the debate over the source, the dispute about Moses’ body being absent from the Old Testament; and the difference over Michael’s identity, Gill reading him as Christ, most as the created archangel. Either way the lesson stands: even the highest holy being uses no insolent speech against the powers of evil, but defers to God’s judgment — exposing how brutish the intruders’ reviling is.
10:
On Jude 10 — “But these speak evil of those things which they know not: but what they know naturally, as brute beasts, in those things they corrupt themselves” — the witnesses mark the brutishness of the intruders.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the two halves. Robertson: “whatsoever things they know not” (cf. 2 Peter 2:12); “brute beasts” (aloga zoa), corrupting themselves. Clarke: they revile what they do not understand, “destitute of reflection.” The Reformed Calvin: they had “no taste but for what was gross… and yet added audacity to madness.”

The verse exposes the double folly of the intruders. They revile the things they do not understand; and the things they do grasp — by mere animal instinct, like brute beasts — they use to corrupt themselves. Ignorance and instinct alike ruin them.

So the witnesses see Jude lay bare the irrational depravity of the false teachers. The witnesses note the contrast with Michael just drawn: where the archangel reverenced what was above him, these men blaspheme heavenly things — angels, dignities, mysteries — that lie wholly beyond their darkened understanding. And in the lower realm, the things they do know, they know not by reason or spiritual sense but by brute instinct, like unreasoning animals; and these very appetites become the instruments of their self-corruption. They mark Calvin’s sharp summary — they relished only what was gross, yet dared condemn what they could not comprehend. The verse thus completes the portrait of verse 8: above, they rail at what they cannot understand; below, they wallow in what they understand only as beasts do. Both ways they prove themselves not spiritual men but creatures of instinct, ruining themselves by the very passions they indulge — a brutishness that calls down the woe Jude pronounces next.

So the witnesses see Jude lay bare the irrational depravity of the false teachers. Where Michael reverenced what was above him, these men blaspheme heavenly things that lie wholly beyond their darkened understanding. And the things they do know, they know not by reason or spiritual sense but by brute instinct, like unreasoning animals; and these very appetites become the instruments of their self-corruption. They mark Calvin’s summary — they relished only what was gross, yet dared condemn what they could not comprehend. Both ways they prove themselves creatures of instinct, ruining themselves by the passions they indulge — the brutishness that calls down the woe Jude pronounces next.
11:
On Jude 11 — “Woe unto them! for they have gone in the way of Cain, and ran greedily after the error of Balaam for reward, and perished in the gainsaying of Core” — the witnesses mark the threefold woe.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three figures. Robertson and Ellicott: “woe” (ouai) echoes Christ’s denunciations; three examples — Cain (Genesis 4:8), Balaam (Numbers 22:7), Korah (Numbers 16:1). Wesley: Cain “the murderer”; “ran greedily… like a torrent” after “the covetous” Balaam; perished in Korah’s rebellion. Clarke: haters of their brethren, corrupting the word for gain, gainsayers of God’s ministers.

The verse pronounces the only “woe” in Jude, with three damning parallels. The intruders walk in Cain’s way, rush after Balaam’s error for gain, and perish in Korah’s rebellion. Three notorious sinners image their sin and its end.

So the witnesses see Jude denounce the false teachers by three Old Testament parallels. The witnesses note the progression: they go “the way of Cain” — the way of envy, hatred of the godly brother, and false worship; they run “greedily after the error of Balaam for reward” — corrupting their office for gain, the covetous prophet’s sin; and they “perish in the gainsaying of Korah” — the rebellion against God’s appointed leaders that ended in the earth swallowing the rebels. They mark that the three together expose the intruders’ envy, greed, and insubordination, and forecast their doom. Wesley notes that Jude alone of the writers here pronounces a “woe.” The verse thus brands the false teachers with the marks of the most infamous rebels of sacred history — murderous like Cain, mercenary like Balaam, mutinous like Korah — and seals their portrait with the certainty of judgment. The three names are a mirror of their character and a prophecy of their ruin, preparing the vivid images of barrenness and disorder that follow.

So the witnesses see Jude denounce the false teachers by three Old Testament parallels. They go “the way of Cain” — envy, hatred of the godly, false worship; they run greedily after “the error of Balaam for reward” — corrupting their office for gain; and they “perish in the gainsaying of Korah” — rebellion against God’s appointed leaders. The three together expose their envy, greed, and insubordination, and forecast their doom. Wesley notes that Jude alone here pronounces a “woe.” The names are a mirror of their character and a prophecy of their ruin — murderous like Cain, mercenary like Balaam, mutinous like Korah.
12:
On Jude 12 — “These are spots in your feasts of charity, when they feast with you, feeding themselves without fear: clouds they are without water, carried about of winds; trees whose fruit withereth, without fruit, twice dead, plucked up by the roots” — the witnesses mark the vivid images of barrenness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figures. Robertson: “hidden rocks” (spilades, reefs covered by water), in the “love-feasts” (agapais); cf. 1 Corinthians 11:17 for disorder. Gill explains the agapae, the love-feasts of the early church. Wesley: “twice dead — in sin, first by nature, and afterwards by apostasy… plucked up by the roots.”

The verse heaps images of danger and emptiness on the intruders. They are hidden reefs at the church’s love-feasts, shamelessly gorging themselves; waterless clouds blown about; fruitless trees in autumn, twice dead and uprooted. All promise, no substance.

So the witnesses see Jude paint the false teachers in a series of striking metaphors. The witnesses note the peril they pose at the very feasts of Christian love: they are “hidden rocks” (or “spots”), submerged reefs on which the unwary are wrecked, feasting without reverence, caring only for themselves. They mark the images of barrenness drawn from nature: “clouds without water,” promising rain but giving none, driven by every wind; “trees whose fruit withereth… twice dead, plucked up by the roots” — dead first by nature and again by apostasy, and so wholly cut off, beyond reviving. The verse thus exposes the intruders’ deceptive show and inner emptiness: they appear within the fellowship, even at its sacred meals, yet contribute nothing but danger and corruption. Like reefs, clouds, and dead trees, they promise much and yield nothing, threatening shipwreck to others and themselves uprooted for the fire. The imagery, continued in the next verse, marks them as hopelessly fruitless and destined for destruction.

So the witnesses see Jude paint the false teachers in a series of striking metaphors. At the very feasts of Christian love they are “hidden rocks,” submerged reefs on which the unwary are wrecked, feasting without reverence, caring only for themselves. They mark the images of barrenness drawn from nature: “clouds without water,” promising rain but giving none; “trees twice dead, plucked up by the roots” — dead by nature and again by apostasy, beyond reviving. They appear within the fellowship, even at its sacred meals, yet contribute nothing but danger and corruption — promising much, yielding nothing, themselves uprooted for the fire.
13:
On Jude 13 — “Raging waves of the sea, foaming out their own shame; wandering stars, to whom is reserved the blackness of darkness for ever” — the witnesses mark the final images and their doom.

The grammatical-historical commentators complete the figures. Robertson: “wild waves” (kymata agria), “foaming out” (epaphrizonta) their shame, recalling Isaiah 57:20; “wandering stars” (planets) reserved “blackness of darkness.” Wesley: stars that “shine for a time, but have no light in themselves, and will be soon cast into utter darkness.” Clarke recalls the “troubled sea… casting up mire and dirt.”

The verse adds two last images and pronounces the intruders’ end. They are wild sea-waves foaming out their own shame, and wandering stars for whom is reserved everlasting darkness. The figures from sea and sky seal their portrait and their doom.

So the witnesses see Jude close his vivid indictment with images from sea and heaven. The witnesses note the “raging waves,” emblem of the intruders’ restless pride and turbulence, casting up the foam of their own shameful deeds as the storm-tossed sea throws up mire and dirt — much sound and fury, nothing but filth. And the “wandering stars” — not the fixed lights by which sailors steer, but erratic ones that mislead, or meteors that flare and vanish — image teachers who have no steady light of their own and lead others astray. They mark the dread sentence attached: for such wandering stars “is reserved the blackness of darkness for ever” — the utter, final darkness of judgment, the fit end of those who forsook the light. The verse thus completes the series of metaphors — reefs, clouds, dead trees, raging waves, wandering stars — drawn, Wesley observes, from air, earth, sea, and heaven, that the intruders’ barren wickedness and certain doom might be set forth on every side. Their show is shame; their portion, everlasting night.

So the witnesses see Jude close his vivid indictment with images from sea and heaven. The “raging waves” are the emblem of restless pride and turbulence, casting up the foam of their own shameful deeds as the storm-tossed sea throws up mire and dirt. And the “wandering stars” — not the fixed lights by which sailors steer, but erratic ones that mislead — image teachers with no steady light of their own who lead others astray. For such is reserved “the blackness of darkness for ever.” The series — reefs, clouds, dead trees, waves, stars, drawn (Wesley) from air, earth, sea, and heaven — sets forth their barren wickedness and certain doom on every side.
14:
On Jude 14 — “And Enoch also, the seventh from Adam, prophesied of these, saying, Behold, the Lord cometh with ten thousands of his saints” — the witnesses mark the prophecy of Enoch.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure and the source. Robertson and Barnes: “Enoch the seventh from Adam” (Genesis 5:18; cf. Hebrews 11:5), so termed in the Book of Enoch; he “prophesied of these” — of just such men. The Reformed Calvin: “I rather think this prophecy was unwritten… delivered down by memory… than taken from an apocryphal book.” Clarke discusses the Book of Enoch.

The verse cites an ancient prophecy against the intruders. Enoch, the seventh from Adam, foretold of such men that the Lord would come with myriads of his holy ones. The doom of the ungodly was announced from the earliest age.

So the witnesses see Jude reach back to the dawn of history for a witness against the false teachers. The witnesses note the antiquity of the testimony: Enoch, the seventh generation from Adam, the man who walked with God and was translated, prophesied of the very kind of ungodly men Jude denounces. They mark the debate over the source — the words resemble a passage in the apocryphal Book of Enoch, and interpreters divide: some, with Clarke, suppose Jude drew the saying from that book; Calvin and others judge it more likely an unwritten prophecy preserved by tradition and now confirmed by the Spirit through Jude, the inspired use guaranteeing its truth whatever its channel. The verse thus shows that the judgment of the ungodly is no novelty but was foretold from the seventh from Adam: the Lord himself will come, attended by ten thousands of his saints, to judge. The very oldest prophecy joins the chorus of warning, announcing the certain advent of the Judge against the men Jude is exposing.

So the witnesses see Jude reach back to the dawn of history for a witness against the false teachers. Enoch, the seventh from Adam, who walked with God and was translated, prophesied of the very kind of ungodly men Jude denounces. They mark the debate over the source — some, with Clarke, suppose Jude drew the saying from the Book of Enoch; Calvin and others judge it an unwritten prophecy preserved by tradition and confirmed by the Spirit through Jude, the inspired use guaranteeing its truth whatever its channel. The judgment of the ungodly is no novelty but was foretold from the seventh from Adam: the Lord will come with ten thousands of his saints to judge.
15:
On Jude 15 — “To execute judgment upon all, and to convince all that are ungodly among them of all their ungodly deeds… and of all their hard speeches which ungodly sinners have spoken against him” — the witnesses mark the substance of the prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the repeated “ungodly.” Robertson: “to do justice” (poiesai krisin), “to convict” (elegxai) the “ungodly” (asebeis, cf. Romans 1:18). Wesley: “convict all the ungodly… of all the grievous things… ungodly sinners have spoken against him.” Calvin: their corrupters did evil “by their wicked life… also by their impure and false speech.”

The verse gives the content of Enoch’s prophecy. The Lord comes to judge all, and to convict the ungodly of all their ungodly deeds and all the hard words spoken against him. Both their works and their speech are arraigned.

So the witnesses see the prophecy press the universality and thoroughness of the coming judgment. The witnesses note the drumbeat of “ungodly” — ungodly men, ungodly deeds, ungodly sinners, hard speeches — hammering home the character of those to be judged. The Lord comes both to “execute judgment” on all and to “convince” or convict the ungodly, leaving them without excuse, of two things: their wicked deeds and their hard, insolent speeches against him. They mark that Jude thus indicts the intruders not only for their corrupt lives but for their proud, blasphemous words — the “great swelling” speeches of verse 16. The judgment will be exhaustive: every deed and every word brought to account. The verse thus completes Enoch’s ancient warning with a comprehensive arraignment of ungodliness in act and speech, sealing the certainty that the railing, licentious teachers Jude denounces will face the Judge for all they have done and said against God. The prophecy from the first age confirms the doom Jude has been declaring.

So the witnesses see the prophecy press the universality and thoroughness of the coming judgment. The drumbeat of “ungodly” — ungodly men, ungodly deeds, ungodly sinners, hard speeches — hammers home the character of those to be judged. The Lord comes both to execute judgment on all and to convict the ungodly, leaving them without excuse, of two things: their wicked deeds and their hard, insolent speeches against him. They mark that Jude indicts the intruders not only for corrupt lives but for proud, blasphemous words. The judgment will be exhaustive — every deed and every word brought to account — the prophecy from the first age confirming the doom Jude declares.
16:
On Jude 16 — “These are murmurers, complainers, walking after their own lusts; and their mouth speaketh great swelling words, having men’s persons in admiration because of advantage” — the witnesses mark the further character of the intruders.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Robertson: “murmurers” (gongystai, as Israel in the wilderness, Numbers 11:1), “complainers” (mempsimoiroi, fault-finders with their lot), “swelling” (hyperonka) words. Wesley: murmurers “against men,” complainers “against God”; admiring men “only for what they can get.” The Puritan Henry: “a murmuring complaining temper… lays men under a very bad character.”

The verse adds to the portrait. The intruders are murmurers and fault-finders, ruled by their own lusts, their mouths uttering pompous words, while they flatter the influential for what they can gain. Discontent, pride, and self-interest mark them.

So the witnesses see Jude detail the temper and tongue of the false teachers. The witnesses note the cluster of vices: they are “murmurers” — like rebellious Israel, grumbling against others and against God’s providence — and “complainers,” perpetually discontented with their lot. Driven by their own lusts, they yet speak “great swelling words,” high-flown and arrogant, much sound and pretension. And beneath the bluster lies cynical self-interest: they “have men’s persons in admiration because of advantage,” flattering the rich and powerful for what they can extract. They mark Henry’s point that a murmuring, complaining spirit betokens a heart at odds with God. The verse thus exposes the inner disposition behind the intruders’ outward sins: a discontented, lustful, proud, and mercenary character, grumbling at providence yet fawning on the influential. It rounds out Jude’s indictment of their lives and prepares the turn to the apostles’ foretelling of just such mockers — and then the warm exhortation to the beloved.

So the witnesses see Jude detail the temper and tongue of the false teachers. They are “murmurers” — like rebellious Israel, grumbling against others and against God’s providence — and “complainers,” perpetually discontented with their lot. Driven by their lusts, they yet speak “great swelling words,” high-flown and arrogant; and beneath the bluster lies cynical self-interest, flattering the powerful for what they can extract. They mark Henry’s point that a murmuring spirit betokens a heart at odds with God. The verse exposes the inner disposition behind their sins — discontented, lustful, proud, and mercenary, grumbling at providence yet fawning on the influential.
17:
On Jude 17 — “But, beloved, remember ye the words which were spoken before of the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the turn to the faithful and the apostolic word.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel and the address. Robertson: “remember ye” (mnesthete), close to 2 Peter 3:2. Barnes notes the striking similarity to 2 Peter 3:1–3. Wesley: “he does not exempt himself from the… apostles,” for next he says “they told you, not us.” Calvin: to the ancient prophecy he adds “the admonitions of the apostles, the memory of whom was recent.”

The verse turns from the intruders to the beloved with a tender “but.” Jude bids them remember the words the apostles had spoken beforehand — the foretelling of just such dangers. The recent apostolic warning is added to Enoch’s ancient one.

So the witnesses see Jude shift, with affectionate address, from denouncing the false to fortifying the faithful. The witnesses note the contrast — “but, beloved” — marking off the true people from the intruders just described. The remedy he offers is memory: let them recall what the apostles of the Lord had plainly foretold. They mark that, having cited the most ancient prophecy (Enoch’s) against the ungodly, he now adds the most recent (the apostles’), so that warning from the first age and the last age alike confirm that such deceivers were to be expected. They observe Wesley’s note that Jude, by saying the apostles “told you,” gently distinguishes himself among or alongside them. The verse thus arms the beloved against alarm and seduction: the appearance of these mockers was no surprise but a fulfilment of apostolic prediction. To remember the word spoken beforehand is to be steadied, not shaken, by the very dangers that prove the apostles true — and prepares the specific prophecy Jude now recalls.

So the witnesses see Jude shift, with affectionate address, from denouncing the false to fortifying the faithful. The contrast — “but, beloved” — marks off the true people from the intruders. The remedy he offers is memory: let them recall what the apostles plainly foretold. Having cited the most ancient prophecy (Enoch’s), he now adds the most recent (the apostles’), so that warnings from the first age and the last alike confirm that such deceivers were to be expected. To remember the word spoken beforehand is to be steadied, not shaken, by the very dangers that prove the apostles true.
18:
On Jude 18 — “How that they told you there should be mockers in the last time, who should walk after their own ungodly lusts” — the witnesses mark the apostolic prediction.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the prophecy. Robertson: declarative “how that” (hoti), close to 2 Peter 3:3. Clarke points to 1 Timothy 4:1; 2 Timothy 3:1; the “last time” the close of the Jewish polity. The Reformed Calvin: the “last time” began “at the first coming of Christ” and runs to the end; the “scoffers” rush into “contempt of God.”

The verse gives the substance of the apostolic warning. They foretold that in the last time mockers would arise, walking after their own ungodly lusts. The intruders are precisely the men the apostles predicted.

So the witnesses see Jude identify the false teachers as the fulfilment of apostolic prophecy. The witnesses note that the apostles had expressly warned of “mockers in the last time” — scoffers who deride godliness and follow their own ungodly desires; and these very men have now appeared. They mark the meaning of “the last time”: the gospel age, begun at Christ’s first coming and continuing to the end, in which such opposition is to be expected. The character of the mockers matches Jude’s intruders exactly — impious scoffers ruled by their lusts. They observe that this prediction, like Enoch’s, both warns and reassures: the rise of such men is no defeat of the gospel but a sign that the apostles spoke truly. The verse thus confirms the beloved in their faith by showing that the present danger was foreseen and foretold. The mockers’ appearance vindicates the apostolic word, and the faithful, forewarned, may stand firm — as the next verse further describes the men, before Jude turns to exhort the saints.

So the witnesses see Jude identify the false teachers as the fulfilment of apostolic prophecy. The apostles had expressly warned of “mockers in the last time” — scoffers who deride godliness and follow their own ungodly desires; and these very men have appeared. They mark the meaning of “the last time”: the gospel age, begun at Christ’s first coming. This prediction, like Enoch’s, both warns and reassures: the rise of such men is no defeat of the gospel but a sign that the apostles spoke truly. The faithful, forewarned, may stand firm — the mockers’ appearance vindicating the apostolic word.
19:
On Jude 19 — “These be they who separate themselves, sensual, having not the Spirit” — the witnesses mark the final mark of the intruders.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three traits. Robertson: “they who make separations” (apodiorizontes), making divisions (cf. 2 Peter 2:1); “sensual” (psychikoi, soulish/animal). Clarke: they separate from the true church “from an affectation of superior wisdom”; “animal… for they have not the Spirit.” The Puritan Henry: “sensual men have not the Spirit” of holiness.

The verse gives the last and deepest mark. These men cause divisions, are merely natural or sensual, and have not the Spirit. The root of all their evil is laid bare: they are without the Holy Spirit.

So the witnesses see Jude reach the heart of the intruders’ condition. The witnesses note the three descriptions, mounting to the decisive one. First, they “separate themselves” — making divisions, drawing parties off from the true church, often, Clarke notes, under pretence of superior wisdom. Second, they are “sensual” (psychikoi) — soulish, governed by the natural appetites, living on the level of the animal, not the spiritual. And third, the root of all: they have “not the Spirit” — the Holy Spirit, the spring of true religion; they are unregenerate, however they profess. They mark that this last is the explanation of all the rest: lacking the Spirit, they cannot but be divisive and sensual. The verse thus exposes the false teachers as, at bottom, men without the Spirit of God — the fatal lack from which their schisms and fleshly lives flow. It completes Jude’s long indictment and throws into bright relief the contrast that follows: against these Spirit-less dividers, the beloved are to build themselves up and pray “in the Holy Ghost.”

So the witnesses see Jude reach the heart of the intruders’ condition. Three descriptions mount to the decisive one. They “separate themselves” — making divisions, drawing parties off, often under pretence of superior wisdom. They are “sensual” (psychikoi) — soulish, governed by natural appetite, living on the animal level. And the root of all: they have “not the Spirit,” the spring of true religion; they are unregenerate, however they profess. Lacking the Spirit, they cannot but be divisive and sensual. The verse exposes the false teachers as, at bottom, men without the Spirit — throwing into bright relief the contrast that follows.
20:
On Jude 20 — “But ye, beloved, building up yourselves on your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Ghost” — the witnesses mark the positive exhortation to the faithful.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure and the contrast. Robertson: “building up” (epoikodomountes), the metaphor of a house (1 Corinthians 3:9; Ephesians 2:20); “most holy faith” the spiritual temple. Clarke: founding all on the gospel; “praying in the Holy Ghost… holding fast the Divine influence.” The Reformed Calvin: the way to overcome Satan’s devices, “love connected with faith,” standing on watch till Christ comes.

The verse turns fully to the beloved with the first of four exhortations. Against the Spirit-less intruders, the faithful are to build themselves up on their most holy faith and to pray in the Holy Ghost. Faith is the foundation, prayer the breath.

So the witnesses see Jude set the saints’ duty in sharp contrast to the false teachers. The witnesses note the emphatic “but ye, beloved” — over against those who divide and lack the Spirit, the faithful have a positive calling. First, they are to be “building up yourselves on your most holy faith” — making the apostolic gospel, the “faith once delivered,” the foundation on which they grow as a holy temple, advancing in grace and knowledge. Second, they are to be “praying in the Holy Ghost” — not in the mere natural spirit (the intruders had “not the Spirit”), but by the Spirit’s own help and influence, the very Spirit the false teachers lacked. They mark the pointed contrast: where the intruders are sensual and Spirit-less, the beloved live by faith and by the Spirit. The verse thus begins the constructive remedy of the whole letter: not merely to contend against error, but to be built up and to pray. The next verse will add the third and fourth duties — keeping in God’s love and looking for Christ’s mercy.

So the witnesses see Jude set the saints’ duty in sharp contrast to the false teachers. Over against those who divide and lack the Spirit, the faithful have a positive calling. First, “building up yourselves on your most holy faith” — making the apostolic gospel, the faith once delivered, the foundation on which they grow as a holy temple. Second, “praying in the Holy Ghost” — by the Spirit’s own help, the very Spirit the false teachers lacked. They mark the pointed contrast: where the intruders are sensual and Spirit-less, the beloved live by faith and by the Spirit. The constructive remedy is not merely to contend against error, but to be built up and to pray.
21:
On Jude 21 — “Keep yourselves in the love of God, looking for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ unto eternal life” — the witnesses mark the third and fourth exhortations.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the responsibility and the hope. Robertson: “keep yourselves” (teresate, aorist of urgency) — in verse 1 they are “kept,” yet here charged to keep (cf. Philippians 2:12). Barnes: “our love to him” is the sense here. The Reformed Calvin: love is “the guardian… of our life… the right course of our calling.” Wesley: keep in “the confident expectation of… eternal life.”

The verse completes the fourfold charge. The beloved are to keep themselves in the love of God, while looking for the mercy of Christ that issues in eternal life. Present perseverance is joined to future hope.

So the witnesses see Jude crown his exhortation with the keeping of love and the looking for mercy. The witnesses note the third duty: “keep yourselves in the love of God” — chiefly, the expositors judge, abiding in our love to God, and so in the sphere of his love to us, persevering in that gracious bond. They mark the beautiful balance with verse 1: there God keeps them, here they are to keep themselves — divine preservation and human responsibility set side by side, as in Paul’s “work out your salvation… for it is God who worketh in you.” The fourth duty is the forward look: “looking for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ unto eternal life” — awaiting, with confident hope, the final mercy of Christ at his coming, which brings eternal life. They note that even the perseverance commanded rests at last on mercy, not merit. The verse thus completes the constructive heart of the letter: built on faith, praying in the Spirit, kept in love, and looking for mercy — the fourfold posture by which the beloved stand secure amid the surrounding apostasy, and are carried safely to eternal life.

So the witnesses see Jude crown his exhortation with the keeping of love and the looking for mercy. The third duty: “keep yourselves in the love of God” — abiding in our love to him, and so in the sphere of his love to us. They mark the balance with verse 1: there God keeps them, here they keep themselves — divine preservation and human responsibility side by side. The fourth is the forward look: “looking for the mercy of Christ unto eternal life,” awaiting his final mercy at his coming. Even the perseverance commanded rests at last on mercy, not merit — the fourfold posture by which the beloved stand secure amid apostasy.
22:
On Jude 22 — “And of some have compassion, making a difference” — the witnesses mark the duty toward the erring.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the discrimination and a textual issue. Robertson: “have mercy” (eleate), though some manuscripts read “refute” (elegchete); the text varies. Barnes: “special and peculiar compassion for a certain class.” Clarke: make “a difference between those… led away by weakness… and those… in the pride… of their heart.” Calvin: treat the erring “in different ways, every one according to his disposition.”

The verse turns from self-keeping to the rescue of others. On some — the wavering and the weak — the faithful are to have compassion, making a difference according to each one’s case. Discernment is to temper mercy.

So the witnesses see Jude direct the saints’ care toward those endangered by the false teachers. The witnesses note that having secured themselves (vv. 20–21), the beloved are not to abandon the erring but to seek their recovery — yet with discrimination, “making a difference” according to the state of each. On some — the wavering, the weak, those led astray through ignorance or pressure of temptation, who are still tractable — they are to have tender compassion, dealing gently to win them back. They mark the textual variation: some manuscripts read “have mercy on,” others “convict” or “reprove,” the precise wording uncertain; but the principle of discernment is clear in either case. The verse thus teaches a wise and graduated pastoral care: not all who err are to be treated alike, but each according to his condition — the gentle and teachable met with compassion. It prepares the more urgent measures of the next verse for those in graver danger, completing Jude’s instruction that the faithful, having kept themselves, are to labour for the salvation of others with both mercy and discernment.

So the witnesses see Jude direct the saints’ care toward those endangered by the false teachers. Having secured themselves, the beloved are not to abandon the erring but to seek their recovery — yet with discrimination, “making a difference” according to each one’s state. On some — the wavering, the weak, led astray through ignorance or temptation, but still tractable — they are to have tender compassion, dealing gently to win them back. They mark the textual variation (“have mercy” / “convict”), but the principle of discernment is clear: not all who err are treated alike, but each according to his condition — the gentle and teachable met with compassion.
23:
On Jude 23 — “And others save with fear, pulling them out of the fire; hating even the garment spotted by the flesh” — the witnesses mark the bolder rescue and the caution.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Robertson: “snatching them out of the fire” (ek pyros harpazontes), recalling Zechariah 3:2; Amos 4:11; the spotted garment to be hated. Barnes: “by appeals adapted to produce fear… the dangers of the persons themselves.” Calvin: avoid “everything that borders on vices,” lest contagion reach the rescuer.

The verse gives the second and bolder mode of rescue, with a warning. Others, in graver peril, are to be saved with fear, snatched as from the fire — while the rescuers hate even the garment defiled by the flesh, lest they be corrupted.

So the witnesses see Jude prescribe a more urgent rescue for those in deeper danger. The witnesses note the escalation from the gentle compassion of verse 22: some are so far gone, so near destruction, that they must be “saved with fear, pulling them out of the fire” — snatched, like a brand from the burning, by solemn and alarming appeals, set before the very flames of judgment. They mark the striking final caution: “hating even the garment spotted by the flesh.” The rescuer must approach the sin-defiled with such abhorrence of their corruption that he shuns even what touches it, like one avoiding a plague-infected garment, lest in saving others he be himself contaminated. The verse thus completes Jude’s pastoral instruction with a vivid double image — the firefighter snatching souls from the flames, and the careful shunning of the very clothing of sin. It teaches a courageous yet self-guarding love: to labour boldly for the perishing, even those most defiled, while keeping a holy fear of contagion. Mercy and discernment, boldness and purity, are joined in the work of saving others.

So the witnesses see Jude prescribe a more urgent rescue for those in deeper danger. Some are so far gone, so near destruction, that they must be “saved with fear, pulling them out of the fire” — snatched, like a brand from the burning, by solemn and alarming appeals. They mark the striking final caution: “hating even the garment spotted by the flesh.” The rescuer must approach the sin-defiled with such abhorrence of their corruption that he shuns even what touches it, like one avoiding a plague-garment, lest in saving others he be himself contaminated. Mercy and discernment, boldness and purity, are joined in the work of saving others.
24:
On Jude 24 — “Now unto him that is able to keep you from falling, and to present you faultless before the presence of his glory with exceeding joy” — the witnesses mark the opening of the great doxology.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the assurance. Robertson: “from stumbling” (aptaistous, sure-footed, not stumbling); “before the presence of his glory” (katenopion tes doxes). Barnes ties it to the dangers of the letter (Romans 16:25). The Puritan Henry: “God is able, and as willing as able, to keep us from falling… not as those who never have been faulty, but” through mercy. Calvin: this shows “our… labors can do nothing except through the power of God.”

The verse opens Jude’s closing doxology with a glorious assurance. To God belongs the power to keep his people from falling and to present them faultless, with exceeding joy, before his glorious presence. He who is the keeper will also be the presenter.

So the witnesses see Jude turn from warning and exhortation to triumphant praise, grounding all in God’s power to keep. The witnesses note the comfort with which the letter ends: amid all the dangers of seduction and apostasy he has described, God is “able to keep you from falling” — to hold his people sure-footed, that they stumble not into the errors and ruin of the ungodly. They mark the breathtaking goal: not merely to preserve them from falling, but to “present them faultless” — spotless, unblameable — before the very presence of his glory, and that “with exceeding joy.” This is the answer to the whole letter’s anxiety: the saints who keep themselves (v. 21) are themselves kept by God, who will bring them home flawless and rejoicing. They note Calvin’s point that human effort avails nothing but through God’s power. The verse thus lifts the reader’s eyes from the present peril to the final glory: the God against whose judgments the ungodly cannot stand is the God whose power will set the faithful, faultless and joyful, before his throne — to whom the next verse ascribes everlasting glory.
25:
On Jude 25 — “To the only wise God our Saviour, be glory and majesty, dominion and power, both now and ever. Amen” — the witnesses mark the closing ascription of praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the doxology. Robertson: “to the only God our Saviour” (mono theo sotheri hemon), cf. Romans 16:27; soter used of God; some texts add “through Jesus Christ our Lord.” Barnes ties it to 1 Timothy 1:17. Gill applies “Saviour” to Christ, “the wisdom of God.” Calvin: “our exhortations and labors can do nothing except through the power of God.”

The verse closes the letter with a full ascription of glory. To the only wise God, the Saviour, Jude ascribes glory, majesty, dominion, and power, now and for ever, sealed with “Amen.” All honour is returned to God.

So the witnesses see Jude end his stern epistle on the highest note of praise. The witnesses note the fulness of the ascription: glory (his honour), majesty (his greatness), dominion and power (his rule and might) — all rendered to God “both now and ever.” They mark the title “the only wise God our Saviour”: against the false teachers’ corrupt wisdom, God alone is truly wise; and he is the Saviour, the Author of the salvation by which the faithful are kept. Some read “Saviour” of Christ, the wisdom of God; the better-attested text adds “through Jesus Christ our Lord,” through whom the praise ascends. They observe how fitly the letter closes: having exposed the ungodly and exhorted the beloved, Jude commits all to the keeping power of God and renders him everlasting glory. The verse thus seals the epistle with worship: the God who judges the ungodly, keeps the faithful, and will present them faultless is the only wise God and Saviour, to whom belong glory, majesty, dominion, and power for ever. “Amen” sets the church’s assent to the praise.

So the witnesses see Jude end his stern epistle on the highest note of praise. The ascription is full: glory, majesty, dominion, and power, all rendered to God “both now and ever.” They mark the title “the only wise God our Saviour”: against the false teachers’ corrupt wisdom, God alone is truly wise, and he is the Author of the salvation by which the faithful are kept. Some read “Saviour” of Christ; the better text adds “through Jesus Christ our Lord.” Having exposed the ungodly and exhorted the beloved, Jude commits all to the keeping power of God and renders him everlasting glory — the “Amen” setting the church’s assent to the praise.
